---
title: "Burner wallets"
subtitle: "Severing the link between identity and on-chain activity."
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status:** Stub. Full chapter coming soon.
:::

## The question

Even with private records, a user's main wallet leaves a public footprint —
the merchant address they paid, the public mapping updates, the gas fees.
For maximum privacy, the user wants a *fresh, ephemeral* address per
transaction, with no link back to their identity.

But a fresh address has no funds. How do you onboard one?
And how do you back it up so the user doesn't lose their money?

## What this chapter will cover

- The burner wallet UX: identity wallet (long-term) + burner wallet (one-shot)
- Funding the burner: two patterns
  - Atomic deposit-and-burn from the identity wallet
  - Cross-chain swap directly into the burner (NEAR Intents, etc.)
- On-chain encrypted backup: storing burner credentials in a record owned by
  the identity wallet, so the user can recover the burner across devices
- The privacy-vs-recoverability trade-off
- NullPay's specific burner implementation (cross-reference to the
  walkthrough chapter)

## Interactive viz planned

A two-actor sequence diagram with toggles:
- Identity wallet ↔ Burner wallet ↔ Merchant
- Show what each observer (chain, merchant, identity-wallet leaker) can see
  with and without the burner pattern

## Why this is hard

Most "privacy wallets" stop at "use a fresh address." But fresh addresses
need funding (which leaks), backup (which centralizes), and UX glue (which
is nontrivial). This chapter walks through the actual deployed pattern.
